title: React Functional Components
tldr: Functional components are plain JavaScript functions that accept props and return JSX.
With hooks (useState, useEffect), functional components can manage state and side effects.
They are simpler, more concise, and easier to test than class components.
Functional components are the modern standard for React development since React 16.8.
laymanDefinition: Think of functional components as LEGO instruction cards. Each card says 'Here are pieces (props), here's what to build (JSX).' Before hooks, you needed complex machines (classes). Now each card can also say 'remember this color' (useState) or 'fetch these pieces' (useEffect).

deepDive.heading: What Is a Functional Component?
deepDive.text: A JavaScript function that accepts props and returns JSX. No this, no render() method, no lifecycle methods - the function IS the render.

deepDive.heading: Hooks - The Game Changer
deepDive.text: React 16.8 introduced hooks: useState for state, useEffect for side effects, useContext for context. Hooks enable stateful logic reuse without classes.

deepDive.heading: Functional vs Class Components
deepDive.text: Functional: less boilerplate, no this binding, easier testing, composition over inheritance. React recommends functional components for all new code.

deepDive.heading: Pure Functional Components
deepDive.text: Should be pure functions of props - same props always return same UI. Side effects go in useEffect, not the function body.

interviewAnswer: Functional components are JS functions that accept props and return JSX. With hooks (useState, useEffect, useReducer), they manage state and side effects, equivalent to class components with less boilerplate. Modern React exclusively uses functional components with hooks.

interviewQuestions.question: What is a functional component?
interviewQuestions.answer: A JS function taking props and returning JSX. With hooks, manages state and side effects.

interviewQuestions.question: Advantages over class components?
interviewQuestions.answer: Less boilerplate, no this binding, easier testing, hooks enable logic reuse, better tree-shaking, smaller bundles.

interviewQuestions.question: Can functional components have state?
interviewQuestions.answer: Yes, via useState: const [count, setCount] = useState(0). Also useReducer for complex state.

interviewQuestions.question: How do they handle side effects?
Why called stateless originally?
interviewQuestions.answer: Before 16.8, they couldn't manage state. Hooks made them fully featured.

interviewQuestions.question: How to optimize against re-renders?
interviewQuestions.answer: React.memo, useMemo, useCallback, useRef, proper dependency arrays.

interviewQuestions.question: What happens on re-render?
interviewQuestions.answer: Entire function re-executes. Hooks persist state via fiber node.

interviewQuestions.question: Can they throw errors?
interviewQuestions.answer: Yes, caught by error boundaries. Hooks must be called before the throw.

codeExamples.title: Counter with useState
codeExamples.description: useState returns state variable and setter. Each setter call triggers re-render.

codeExamples.title: Props with Defaults
codeExamples.description: Destructure props with defaults. Component is pure function of props.

codeExamples.title: Data Fetching Pattern
codeExamples.description: Cleanup flag prevents setState on unmounted component.

mcqQuestions.question: What is a functional component?
options: JS function taking props returning JSX
mcqQuestions.explanation: Plain JS function with props and JSX return.

mcqQuestions.question: How do functional components manage state?
mcqQuestions.explanation: useState adds state to functional components.

mcqQuestions.question: Benefit over class components?
options: Less boilerplate, no this, hooks reuse

mcqQuestions.question: When do they re-render?
mcqQuestions.explanation: Re-render on state, props, or parent change.

mcqQuestions.question: Can they throw errors?
mcqQuestions.explanation: Errors caught by boundaries; hooks unconditional.
